questions.stem: Your developer-productivity agent needs to find every caller of a function named `processPayment` across a large codebase. Which built-in tool is the most appropriate starting point?

options.text: Use Grep to search file contents across the codebase for `processPayment`.
options.explanation: Grep is for content search — finding patterns like function names or callers across files — which is exactly this task.

options.text: Use Glob to match files named `processPayment`.
options.explanation: Glob matches file paths/names, not occurrences of a symbol inside file contents.

options.text: Use Read on every single file in the whole repository and scan each one for the name.
options.explanation: Reading all files upfront is wasteful; Grep targets the content directly.

options.text: Use Bash to delete and regenerate the files until the references appear.
options.explanation: Nonsensical and destructive; unrelated to searching for callers.

questions.stem: You want a shared MCP server available to the whole team via version control, authenticated with a GitHub token that must not be committed. How should you configure it?

options.text: Use project-scoped `.mcp.json` and reference the token via `${GITHUB_TOKEN}`.
options.explanation: Project-scoped `.mcp.json` shares the server via version control, and environment variable expansion supplies credentials without committing secrets.

options.text: Configure it in the user-scoped `~/.claude.json` file with the GitHub token pasted inline so it travels with you.
options.explanation: User-scoped config isn't shared with the team, and inline tokens risk credential leakage.

options.text: Commit the GitHub token directly into the shared `.mcp.json` file so every teammate has it on checkout.
options.explanation: Committing a secret is a serious security violation.

options.text: Hardcode the token into each developer's shell profile and skip MCP config.
options.explanation: This doesn't configure the shared server and scatters secrets across machines.

questions.stem: You are implementing an agentic loop by hand against the Claude API. Which condition should terminate the loop?

options.text: Loop while `stop_reason` is `tool_use`, then terminate once it is `end_turn`.
options.explanation: The agentic loop lifecycle keys off `stop_reason`: keep iterating on `tool_use`, terminate on `end_turn`. This is the reliable, model-driven control signal.

options.text: Parse the assistant's natural-language reply text for phrases such as "I'm done" or "all finished" and stop the loop the moment one of them appears.
options.explanation: Parsing natural-language signals is an anti-pattern — unreliable compared to the structured `stop_reason`.

options.text: Set a fixed maximum-iteration cap as the primary stopping mechanism regardless of what the model reports.
options.explanation: Arbitrary iteration caps as the primary stop are an anti-pattern; they cut off legitimate work or run too long.

options.text: Stop as soon as any assistant message contains text content.
options.explanation: Text content can accompany tool-use turns; it isn't a valid completion indicator.

questions.stem: You ask the agent to list all React test files so it can run them. Which built-in tool and pattern is correct for locating files like `**/*.test.tsx`?

options.text: Run a Bash loop that cats each directory and filters by extension.
options.explanation: Shelling out is fragile and slower; Glob is purpose-built for path patterns.

options.text: Use Glob with the pattern `**/*.test.tsx` to match the file paths by name.
options.explanation: Glob matches file paths and names by pattern, so `**/*.test.tsx` is the right way to enumerate those files.

options.text: Use Grep for the string `.test.tsx` across the contents of all files.
options.explanation: Grep searches file contents, not file names; it would miss files and match unrelated text.

options.text: Use Read on the project root and parse the directory listing manually.
options.explanation: Read operates on individual files, not directory trees, so it cannot enumerate matches.

questions.stem: The agent tries to make a targeted Edit to a config file but the operation fails because the matched text appears multiple times. What is the recommended fallback?

options.text: Retry the same Edit several more times until one of the repeated matches happens to take effect.
options.explanation: Repeating an Edit on non-unique text keeps failing; it never resolves the ambiguity.

options.text: Switch to Glob to relocate the file before attempting the edit again.
options.explanation: Glob finds file paths; it does nothing to resolve ambiguous text inside a known file.

options.text: Read the file and use Write to replace the whole file with the corrected content.
options.explanation: When Edit fails on non-unique text, the recommended fallback is Read then Write to rewrite the full file with the change.

options.text: Use Bash sed in place since Edit cannot handle repeated text at all.
options.explanation: Bash sed is error-prone for structured edits; the documented fallback is Read plus Write.

questions.stem: An engineer wants the agent to understand an unfamiliar module efficiently rather than dumping every file into context. What is the most effective approach?

options.text: Read every file in the module up front so the full picture is in context.
options.explanation: Reading everything wastes context and degrades performance on irrelevant content.

options.text: Grep for key symbols to find entry points, then Read only the relevant files.
options.explanation: Building understanding incrementally — Grep to locate relevant code, then Read just those files — is the efficient documented pattern.

options.text: Glob the entire module tree and then Read the first ten matching files in alphabetical order.
options.explanation: Reading arbitrary files by name order does not target the relevant code.

options.text: Ask Bash to print line counts so the agent reads the largest files first.
options.explanation: File size is not a proxy for relevance; this still reads unneeded content.

questions.stem: The agent needs to create a brand-new utility file that does not exist yet. Which built-in tool is appropriate?

options.text: Edit, supplying the entire new contents as the replacement string for an empty match.
options.explanation: Edit requires existing unique text to match; it cannot create a file from nothing.

options.text: Grep, redirecting its search output into the destination path.
options.explanation: Grep searches content and produces no file; it cannot author new files.

options.text: Write, which performs a full-file operation to create the file.
options.explanation: Write performs full-file operations and is the correct tool for creating a new file with its complete contents.

options.text: Glob, since matching the intended path is enough to create it.
options.explanation: Glob only matches existing paths; it neither creates nor writes files.

questions.stem: An engineer needs the agent to change a single uniquely-identifiable line in a 4,000-line file without rewriting the whole file. Which tool fits best?

options.text: Use Edit with a unique text match to make the targeted change in place.
options.explanation: Edit makes targeted edits by matching unique text, which is ideal for changing one line without rewriting the file.

options.text: Use Write to output the whole file with the one line changed.
options.explanation: Write rewrites the whole file, which is wasteful when a targeted Edit will do.

options.text: Use Grep to replace the matched line directly in the file contents.
options.explanation: Grep only searches; it does not modify files.

options.text: Use Glob to find the line by its pattern and apply the change there.
options.explanation: Glob matches file paths, not lines within a file, and cannot edit.

questions.stem: Production logs show an agent run timing out while it repeatedly reads large files trying to locate where an env var `STRIPE_KEY` is consumed. What is the most effective first step?

options.text: Read each service file fully in sequence until the usage is spotted.
options.explanation: Sequential full reads are exactly the slow pattern causing the timeout.

options.text: Grep for `STRIPE_KEY` to pinpoint the files, then Read only those matches.
options.explanation: Grep locates the symbol quickly across the repo so only the relevant files get Read, avoiding wasteful full reads.

options.text: Glob for files containing `stripe` in the name and read all matches.
options.explanation: Filename matching misses files where the var is used but the name differs.

options.text: Write a temporary index file cataloguing every variable first.
options.explanation: Manually building an index is more work than a direct content search.

questions.stem: A developer wants to experiment with a personal MCP server on their own machine without affecting teammates or the repo. Where should it be configured?

options.text: In the project `.mcp.json` so it stays close to the related code.
options.explanation: Project `.mcp.json` is committed and shared with the whole team, not personal.

options.text: In the user-level config (`~/.claude.json`) so it stays personal to them.
options.explanation: User-level config is for personal or experimental servers that apply to your machine without being shared via version control.

options.text: Inside the committed project `CLAUDE.md` file so that Claude reads the server at the start of each session.
options.explanation: CLAUDE.md provides instructions, not MCP server configuration, and would be shared.

options.text: In a new `.github/workflows` file so CI picks the server up too.
options.explanation: Workflow files configure CI, not a local personal MCP server.

questions.stem: Your team needs a standard integration with GitHub for the agent. What is the recommended approach before writing any custom tooling?

options.text: Author a separate bespoke tool for every single GitHub API endpoint the team could conceivably need.
options.explanation: Hand-authoring tools for every endpoint is the maintenance burden MCP exists to avoid.

options.text: Prefer an existing community or official MCP server for the standard integration.
options.explanation: For standard integrations, reusing an existing community or official MCP server avoids writing and maintaining bespoke tool code.

options.text: Have the agent call the GitHub REST API via raw Bash curl every time.
options.explanation: Raw curl scatters undocumented commands and lacks proper tool schemas.

options.text: Embed all GitHub logic inside the project CLAUDE.md as instructions.
options.explanation: CLAUDE.md gives guidance but does not provide executable tools or integration.

questions.stem: An MCP server exposes the production database schema as a resource. How does this most help the agent's workflow?

options.text: It lets the agent execute schema migrations automatically against production without any human review step.
options.explanation: Resources expose read-only content; they do not execute migrations.

options.text: It grants write access to the database tables through the resource URI.
options.explanation: Resources are read-only content catalogs, not write paths.

options.text: It exposes the schema as content the agent can read, reducing exploratory tool calls.
options.explanation: MCP resources expose content catalogs like schemas and docs, so the agent can read them directly instead of making many exploratory tool calls.

options.text: It replaces the need for any CLAUDE.md documentation in the repo.
options.explanation: Resources complement project memory; they do not replace CLAUDE.md guidance.

questions.stem: After adding a new MCP server to `.mcp.json` and reconnecting, when does the agent become aware of that server's tools?

options.text: Tools from all configured MCP servers are discovered at connection time.
options.explanation: Tools from every configured MCP server are discovered when the connection is established, making them available to the agent.

options.text: Only after each of the server's tools is individually re-declared by hand inside the project CLAUDE.md.
options.explanation: MCP tools are discovered automatically; you do not redeclare them in CLAUDE.md.

options.text: Only once the user manually types each tool name into the prompt.
options.explanation: Discovery is automatic at connection; users need not name tools manually.

options.text: Never, until the project is rebuilt and the CLI binary reinstalled.
options.explanation: No rebuild or reinstall is needed; reconnecting discovers the tools.

questions.stem: A teammate reports that a shared MCP server defined in `.mcp.json` fails to authenticate on their machine, though the config references `${JIRA_TOKEN}`. What is the most likely root cause?

options.text: Environment variable expansion is unsupported inside `.mcp.json`, so the referenced token can never be resolved at all.
options.explanation: Environment variable expansion is supported; that is the intended mechanism.

options.text: The teammate has not set the `JIRA_TOKEN` environment variable in their shell.
options.explanation: The committed config references `${JIRA_TOKEN}` by design; each developer must supply their own value via the environment variable, which the teammate has not set.

options.text: The token must be committed inline in `.mcp.json` to authenticate at all.
options.explanation: Committing secrets is wrong; expansion exists precisely to avoid that.

options.text: Shared servers can only ever live in user-level `~/.claude.json`.
options.explanation: Shared servers belong in project `.mcp.json`; user config is for personal servers.

questions.stem: Two MCP server scopes are available: project-level `.mcp.json` and user-level `~/.claude.json`. Which statement best captures the trade-off?

options.text: Both scopes are identical except that one uses JSON and one uses YAML.
options.explanation: Both are JSON; the real difference is sharing scope, not format.

options.text: User-level config is committed and shared with the whole team, while project-level config stays private to you.
options.explanation: This reverses the truth: project-level is shared, user-level is private.

options.text: Project-level config is committed and shared; user-level is personal to your machine.
options.explanation: Project `.mcp.json` is checked in for shared team tooling, while user-level `~/.claude.json` holds personal or experimental servers for your machine only.

options.text: Project-level config can hold secrets safely while user-level cannot.
options.explanation: Neither scope should hold raw secrets; both rely on env var expansion.

questions.stem: A docs MCP server exposes a catalog of library documentation as resources. Why prefer reading that resource over having the agent Grep the codebase for usage examples?

options.text: The built-in Grep tool is automatically disabled whenever any MCP server is connected to the current session.
options.explanation: Grep remains available; MCP servers do not disable built-in tools.

options.text: The resource provides authoritative content directly, cutting exploratory tool calls.
options.explanation: Resources expose curated documentation as content, so the agent reads authoritative material instead of inferring usage from many exploratory searches.

options.text: Resources execute the library code so the agent sees real runtime output.
options.explanation: Resources expose content, not executed code or runtime output.

options.text: Grep cannot search any file that a library has installed under node_modules.
options.explanation: Grep can search those files; the advantage is reduced exploration, not a Grep limitation.

questions.stem: When running the agent in CI (e.g. GitHub Actions) with an MCP server, how must tool permissions be handled compared to local development?

options.text: Each tool from each MCP server must be listed explicitly in allowed tools.
options.explanation: Unlike local development, CI has no permission shortcut: every tool from every MCP server must be individually listed in the allowed tools.

options.text: Permissions are inherited automatically from the repository settings, exactly as they are during local development.
options.explanation: CI does not auto-inherit tool permissions from repo settings.

options.text: A single blanket permission covers every MCP operation in CI runs.
options.explanation: There is no blanket permission; tools must be enumerated.

options.text: No permissions are needed because CI is a trusted environment.
options.explanation: CI still requires explicit allow-listing of each tool.

questions.stem: A custom MCP tool named `query_db` is frequently misused by the agent, which passes free-text questions instead of SQL. The schema is correct. What should you improve first?

options.text: Rename the tool to `db` so the agent thinks less about what it does.
options.explanation: A shorter name gives the model less, not more, guidance about correct use.

options.text: Lower the model's effort level so that it spends fewer reasoning tokens deciding how to make the call.
options.explanation: Effort level does not fix an ambiguous tool contract.

options.text: Sharpen the tool description and parameter descriptions to state SQL is required.
options.explanation: Clear, specific tool and parameter descriptions are what guide the model to call a tool correctly; stating that a valid SQL string is required fixes the misuse.

options.text: Add three more parameters so the agent has more options to choose from.
options.explanation: More parameters add confusion; the issue is unclear guidance, not missing inputs.

questions.stem: When defining an MCP tool with the Python SDK, what is the role of the `Field(description=...)` on each parameter?

options.text: It encrypts the parameter value before the tool function receives it.
options.explanation: Field descriptions do not encrypt anything; they document the parameter.

options.text: It helps the model understand what each parameter expects so it can call the tool correctly.
options.explanation: Parameter field descriptions tell the model what each argument means, which is essential for the model to call the tool correctly.

options.text: It sets a default value used whenever the model omits the parameter.
options.explanation: A description is documentation, not a default-value mechanism.

options.text: It registers the parameter as a separate standalone MCP resource exposed under its own URI.
options.explanation: Descriptions do not create resources; they annotate tool parameters.

questions.stem: Your agent has many MCP servers connected, and `/context` shows tool definitions consuming a large share of the window before any work begins. What is the most effective remedy?

options.text: Disable the MCP servers not relevant to the current task to free context.
options.explanation: MCP servers load all their tool definitions into context even when unused, so disabling irrelevant servers reclaims that space.

options.text: Increase the session's effort level so the model can reason its way past all of the clutter.
options.explanation: Effort level does not reduce the context cost of tool definitions.

options.text: Read fewer project files so the tool definitions take relatively less.
options.explanation: Reading fewer files does not shrink the always-loaded tool definitions.

options.text: Commit the tool definitions to CLAUDE.md so they load only once.
options.explanation: Putting definitions in CLAUDE.md would add context, not save it.

questions.stem: A tool has a CLI equivalent like `gh` for GitHub. Why might invoking the CLI through Bash be more context-efficient than the equivalent MCP server?

options.text: The CLI runs faster because it is compiled and the MCP server is interpreted.
options.explanation: Execution speed is not the context argument; persistent definitions are.

options.text: The CLI returns smaller results that the model never has to read at all.
options.explanation: CLI output still enters context; the saving is on tool definitions, not results.

options.text: The CLI does not add any persistent tool definitions to the context window upfront.
options.explanation: An MCP server loads tool definitions into context persistently; a CLI invoked via Bash adds no such definitions, so it is more context-efficient.

options.text: The CLI bypasses the agentic loop and answers the user directly itself.
options.explanation: The CLI runs through the Bash tool inside the loop; it does not bypass it.

questions.stem: If your MCP tools exceed roughly 10% of the context window, what does Claude Code do automatically?

options.text: It silently disconnects the least-recently-used MCP server until total usage falls back below 10%.
options.explanation: It does not drop servers; it changes how tools are surfaced.

options.text: It switches to tool search mode, discovering the right tools on demand.
options.explanation: When MCP tools exceed about 10% of the window, Claude Code switches to tool search mode, discovering tools on demand rather than loading them all.

options.text: It compacts the entire conversation history to make room for tools.
options.explanation: Compaction summarizes conversation, not tool definitions, and is a different trigger.

options.text: It refuses to start the session until servers are manually disabled.
options.explanation: The session still starts; it just adopts tool search mode.

questions.stem: An architect wants frequently-loaded capabilities that do not consume context up front the way MCP tool definitions do. Which option fits that goal?

options.text: Skills, which keep only a name and description until needed.
options.explanation: Skills keep just a name and description in context and load the full contents only when needed, unlike MCP servers that load all tool definitions upfront.

options.text: Adding every server to `.mcp.json` so they share one definition block.
options.explanation: More servers in `.mcp.json` add more definitions, increasing context cost.

options.text: Raising the effort level so the model tolerates larger tool payloads.
options.explanation: Effort level has no effect on how tool definitions consume context.

options.text: Pasting the tool schemas directly into the user prompt each session.
options.explanation: Pasting schemas into the prompt adds context rather than saving it.

questions.stem: The agent must replace a config value but the surrounding text it picked is not unique in the file. Which sequence avoids the failure while still making a precise change?

options.text: Expand the matched text to include enough surrounding context to be unique, then Edit.
options.explanation: Edit requires a unique match, so widening the snippet with surrounding context makes it unique and lets the targeted Edit succeed.

options.text: Glob the file repeatedly until the duplicate lines are filtered out.
options.explanation: Glob matches paths, not lines, and cannot disambiguate text.

options.text: Grep the value and let Grep overwrite the first occurrence it finds.
options.explanation: Grep does not modify files; it only searches content.

options.text: Read the file and then report back to the user that this particular value cannot be edited safely at all.
options.explanation: The edit is achievable by making the match unique or by Read plus Write.

questions.stem: An engineer asks the agent to find all TODO comments and also list every YAML file in the repo. Which pairing of tools is correct?

options.text: Glob for the TODO comments inside file content, and Grep for the `**/*.yaml` file paths by name.
options.explanation: This swaps the tools: Glob does not search content and Grep does not match paths.

options.text: Grep for `TODO` across file contents, and Glob for `**/*.yaml` file paths.
options.explanation: Grep searches contents (the TODO comments) while Glob matches file paths by pattern (the YAML files); each tool is used for its purpose.

options.text: Read every file for TODOs, and Read the directory tree for YAML files.
options.explanation: Read works on single files and cannot enumerate matches across a tree.

options.text: Bash grep for both tasks since the built-in tools cannot do either job.
options.explanation: The built-in Grep and Glob handle these tasks directly; Bash is unnecessary.

questions.stem: The agent has the file path and wants the complete current contents of a 50-line module before reasoning about a refactor. Which tool should it use?

options.text: Edit, since reading a file and editing it are really the same underlying operation.
options.explanation: Edit modifies text; it is not for retrieving full contents.

options.text: Glob, since matching the path returns the file's full contents.
options.explanation: Glob returns matching paths, not the contents of files.

options.text: Read, which performs a full-file operation to return the contents.
options.explanation: Read is the full-file operation for retrieving a file's complete contents, which is what the refactor reasoning needs.

options.text: Grep, since searching for any character returns the whole file.
options.explanation: Grep returns matching lines with context, not a guaranteed full-file read.

questions.stem: A team standardizes on a Playwright MCP server for browser-driven UI checks. What is the cleanest way to ensure every teammate gets it automatically?

options.text: Email teammates the `claude mcp add` command to run individually.
options.explanation: Manual per-developer setup is error-prone and not automatic.

options.text: Define it in the committed project `.mcp.json` so it is shared on checkout.
options.explanation: A project-scoped `.mcp.json` under version control gives everyone on the codebase the same server automatically when they check it out.

options.text: Add it to each person's user-level `~/.claude.json` together over a guided screen-share session.
options.explanation: User-level config is personal and not shared with the team.

options.text: Document the install steps in a wiki and ask everyone to follow them.
options.explanation: A wiki relies on manual action; committed config is automatic.

questions.stem: A team coding convention (use server actions, not API routes) should apply to everyone on the project. Where should it live so the whole team benefits via source control?

options.text: In the user-level `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md`, which applies to every project on your own machine.
options.explanation: User-level memory is personal to your machine and is not shared with the team.

options.text: In the project root `CLAUDE.md`, which is committed and shared with the team.
options.explanation: Project-root CLAUDE.md is committed to source control and shared, so team conventions belong there.

options.text: In `CLAUDE.local.md`, which holds personal, uncommitted instructions.
options.explanation: CLAUDE.local.md is not shared with other engineers; it is for personal customizations.

options.text: In a custom slash command file under `.claude/commands/`.
options.explanation: Commands automate workflows; they are not the place for always-on conventions.

questions.stem: You want certain instructions to apply across every project you personally work on, regardless of the repo. Which file is appropriate?

options.text: The project root `CLAUDE.md`, committed separately in each and every repository you happen to touch.
options.explanation: Project-root files apply per repo and are shared, not personal across all projects.

options.text: A `.claude/rules/` file added separately to every repository you use.
options.explanation: Per-repo rules files do not span all your projects automatically.

options.text: The user-level `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md`, used across all projects on your machine.
options.explanation: The user-level `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md` applies to all projects on your machine, which is exactly the personal cross-project scope.

options.text: A `CLAUDE.local.md` placed once in your home directory's root.
options.explanation: CLAUDE.local.md is a per-project, uncommitted file, not a global personal one.

questions.stem: Your repo already has an `AGENTS.md` for another tool and you do not want to duplicate its content into `CLAUDE.md`. What is the recommended approach?

options.text: Copy the full AGENTS.md text into CLAUDE.md and then keep both copies in sync manually by hand.
options.explanation: Manual duplication is exactly what the @import syntax avoids.

options.text: Rename AGENTS.md to CLAUDE.md so Claude reads it as project memory.
options.explanation: Renaming breaks the other tool that relies on AGENTS.md.

options.text: Add `@AGENTS.md` on the first line of CLAUDE.md to import its contents.
options.explanation: The @import syntax (`@AGENTS.md` on the first line of CLAUDE.md) loads that file's contents first, avoiding duplication while keeping Claude-specific notes below.

options.text: Configure the import inside the project `.mcp.json` server block.
options.explanation: `.mcp.json` configures servers, not memory-file imports.

questions.stem: You added a new directory-level `CLAUDE.md` and edited the project root one, but suspect the agent is not loading what you expect. How do you verify which memory files are active?

options.text: Run the `/memory` command to see and verify exactly which memory files are loaded.
options.explanation: The `/memory` command lets you view and verify which CLAUDE.md files are currently loaded into the session.

options.text: Run `/compact` and read the summary to infer which files loaded.
options.explanation: Compaction summarizes conversation; it does not report loaded memory files.

options.text: Delete the root file and watch whether the agent's behavior changes.
options.explanation: Destructive trial-and-error is unnecessary when `/memory` reports the truth.

options.text: Inspect `.mcp.json` to confirm the memory files are registered.
options.explanation: Memory files are not registered in `.mcp.json`; that file configures servers.

questions.stem: An engineer keeps personal scratch notes and shortcuts that should never reach teammates. Which file should hold them?

options.text: The committed project root `CLAUDE.md` so they are versioned safely.
options.explanation: The root file is shared with the team, so personal notes do not belong there.

options.text: `CLAUDE.local.md`, which holds personal instructions and is not shared.
options.explanation: CLAUDE.local.md is the uncommitted, personal file meant for instructions you do not share with other engineers.

options.text: A `.claude/rules/` file committed alongside the team's shared rules.
options.explanation: Committed rules files are shared; they are not for personal-only notes.

options.text: The user-level config file so that the personal notes follow them into every single project they open.
options.explanation: User-level memory spans all projects, which is broader than per-project personal notes.

questions.stem: A database schema file is relevant to many tasks, and you want its contents available without the agent searching for it each time. How should you wire this into project memory?

options.text: Paste the entire schema file's text directly into the body of CLAUDE.md and maintain it there.
options.explanation: Pasting the full schema bloats CLAUDE.md and drifts out of sync with the source.

options.text: Reference it with `@prisma/schema.prisma` so its contents load each request.
options.explanation: Using @import in CLAUDE.md pulls the file's contents into every request, so the agent can answer schema questions without searching.

options.text: Add the schema path to the deny list in `.claude/settings.json`.
options.explanation: A deny rule blocks access; it does not preload the file.

options.text: Expose it only through a custom slash command the user must invoke.
options.explanation: A command requires manual invocation; @import makes the content always available.

questions.stem: When two CLAUDE.md instructions conflict — one in the user-level file and one in the project root file — which guidance reflects the intended hierarchy for a project?

options.text: Project-level memory governs project work; user-level holds personal defaults.
options.explanation: Project-level CLAUDE.md captures project-specific, team-shared guidance, while user-level holds your personal cross-project defaults that the project context refines.

options.text: User-level always wins because it is loaded earliest in the session.
options.explanation: Load order does not make user-level override project-specific guidance for a project.

options.text: Whichever file is larger in bytes is treated as authoritative by Claude.
options.explanation: File size has no bearing on precedence.

options.text: Both memory files are ignored entirely unless the same instruction is also repeated inside a slash command.
options.explanation: Memory files are honored without needing a slash command to restate them.

questions.stem: An engineer is about to ask the agent for a multi-file feature touching an unfamiliar pipeline and wants to review the approach before any code is written. Which mode fits best?

options.text: Plan mode, which reads files and returns a reviewable plan before executing.
options.explanation: Plan mode uses read-only tools to research the codebase and produce a plan you approve before any edits, ideal for broad multi-file work.

options.text: Direct execution with auto-accept so edits land as fast as possible.
options.explanation: Auto-accept skips the review step the engineer explicitly wants.

options.text: Raising the effort level, which produces the plan and then immediately applies it all at once without review.
options.explanation: Effort level controls reasoning depth, not the plan-then-approve gate.

options.text: A custom slash command that commits the change without a plan step.
options.explanation: A commit command does not provide the pre-implementation review requested.

questions.stem: Choosing between plan mode and a higher effort level: which framing best describes when to use each?

options.text: Plan mode is strictly for making fast edits, whereas a higher effort level is strictly for slow and careful edits.
options.explanation: Neither is primarily about edit speed; they address breadth versus depth.

options.text: Plan mode handles breadth across the codebase; higher effort handles depth on hard logic.
options.explanation: Plan mode suits multi-step, multi-file breadth, while a higher effort level suits deep reasoning on complex logic or debugging.

options.text: Plan mode disables tools entirely; higher effort enables every tool.
options.explanation: Plan mode still uses read-only tools; effort level does not toggle tool access.

options.text: Plan mode is cheaper in tokens; higher effort is always free to use.
options.explanation: Both consume extra tokens; cost is not the distinguishing factor here.

questions.stem: An engineer only wants a summary of where authentication lives, with the verbose file-by-file discovery kept out of the main conversation. What is the best approach?

options.text: Read every candidate auth file directly in the main thread and skim through all of the output yourself.
options.explanation: Reading in the main thread is exactly the verbose clutter to avoid.

options.text: Use the Explore subagent so discovery runs in isolation and returns a summary.
options.explanation: The Explore subagent runs the verbose exploration in its own context and returns just a summary, keeping the main context clean.

options.text: Enter plan mode and approve the plan to surface the discovery inline.
options.explanation: Plan mode is for planning changes; here no change is intended, just a summary.

options.text: Run `/compact` first so the discovery output is summarized afterward.
options.explanation: Compaction acts after the fact; it does not isolate discovery up front.

questions.stem: While reviewing a generated plan, an engineer notices a missing edge case. What is the appropriate action before any code is written?

options.text: Approve the plan as written and then circle back to fix the missing edge case in a follow-up prompt later.
options.explanation: Approving locks in the gap; the pre-code review is the cheapest place to fix it.

options.text: Revise the plan (or edit it) so the edge case is covered before approving.
options.explanation: Plan mode exists precisely so you can course-correct before code is written; revise or edit the plan to cover the edge case, then approve.

options.text: Switch to a higher effort level and resubmit the same prompt verbatim.
options.explanation: Effort level does not address a specific missing requirement in the plan.

options.text: Clear the session so the planner forgets the flawed first attempt.
options.explanation: Clearing discards useful context; revising the plan is the targeted fix.

questions.stem: Your team runs the same boilerplate-generation steps constantly with project-specific conventions. What is the cleanest way to standardize this into one repeatable invocation?

options.text: Paste the full instructions into the prompt manually on each run.
options.explanation: Manual pasting is the repetition a custom command eliminates.

options.text: Create a custom slash command markdown file under `.claude/commands/`.
options.explanation: A markdown file in `.claude/commands/` becomes a slash command, turning the repeated workflow with its conventions into one reusable invocation.

options.text: Add the steps to `.mcp.json` so a server runs them automatically.
options.explanation: `.mcp.json` configures servers, not reusable prompt workflows.

options.text: Define a PreToolUse hook that emits the boilerplate instructions before each tool call runs.
options.explanation: Hooks intercept tool calls; they are not an on-demand workflow command.

questions.stem: A custom command should accept a file path the engineer passes at runtime, like `/write_tests the use-auth.ts file`. How do you make it accept that input?

options.text: Include the `$ARGUMENTS` placeholder in the command's markdown file.
options.explanation: The `$ARGUMENTS` placeholder in the command file captures whatever the user passes at runtime, making the command reusable with different inputs.

options.text: Add a `@parameters` decorator at the very top of the command's markdown file.
options.explanation: There is no `@parameters` decorator for custom commands.

options.text: Declare the parameters inside `.claude/settings.json` permissions.
options.explanation: Settings configure permissions and hooks, not command arguments.

options.text: Append command-line flags when invoking the command in the prompt.
options.explanation: Arguments flow through `$ARGUMENTS`, not CLI flags typed into the prompt.

questions.stem: After creating a new file `audit.md` in `.claude/commands/`, what must the engineer do for the `/audit` command to become available?

options.text: Nothing extra; Claude Code picks the command up automatically without a restart.
options.explanation: After creating the command file, Claude Code detects it automatically and no restart is required.

options.text: Restart Claude Code so it rescans the commands directory on launch.
options.explanation: No restart is needed; the command is picked up automatically.

options.text: Register the command name inside `.claude/settings.json` first.
options.explanation: Commands are not registered in settings; the filename defines the command.

options.text: Run `/init` once again so that Claude regenerates its entire command index from scratch on launch.
options.explanation: `/init` analyzes the codebase for CLAUDE.md; it is unrelated to command discovery.

questions.stem: An engineer wants a capability with a name and short description loaded into context, where the full instructions load only when Claude decides to use it. Which mechanism is that?

options.text: A Skill, whose full contents load on demand only when Claude needs it.
options.explanation: A Skill keeps only its name and description in context and loads the full instructions only when Claude determines it should use it.

options.text: An MCP server, which loads only metadata until the first tool call.
options.explanation: MCP servers load all their tool definitions into context upfront, not lazily.

options.text: A CLAUDE.md @import, which streams its target file piece by piece.
options.explanation: An @import loads the full referenced file into every request, not on demand.

options.text: A PostToolUse hook, which injects instructions after each tool call.
options.explanation: Hooks run commands around tool calls; they are not lazily-loaded capabilities.

questions.stem: A team wants the full commit, push, and PR-creation flow handled in one step instead of three manual commands. Which built-in feature does this?

options.text: The `/commit-push-pr` skill, which handles all three steps in one invocation.
options.explanation: The `/commit-push-pr` skill performs the commit, push, and PR creation together in a single step.

options.text: A PreToolUse hook matched to the Bash tool running git commands.
options.explanation: A hook intercepts tool calls; it does not orchestrate the full PR workflow on request.

options.text: The `/init` command, which sets up the repository for committing.
options.explanation: `/init` generates a CLAUDE.md; it does not commit or open PRs.

options.text: An MCP resource that exposes the full git history and pending diff as readable content for review.
options.explanation: A resource exposes content; it does not perform commits or PRs.

questions.stem: An engineer keeps correcting the agent to never add API routes. To stop the repetition across future sessions, what is the most durable fix?

options.text: Repeat the correction at the start of every new session manually.
options.explanation: Manual repetition each session is the toil a memory file removes.

options.text: Save the rule to CLAUDE.md so it loads automatically next session.
options.explanation: Persisting the correction in CLAUDE.md means the agent reads it at the start of every session, preventing the repeated mistake.

options.text: Lower the effort level so the agent makes fewer creative choices.
options.explanation: Effort level does not encode a specific project rule.

options.text: Disable the Write tool entirely so that the agent cannot create any API route files at all.
options.explanation: Disabling Write blocks legitimate edits too; a memory rule is the right scope.

questions.stem: A task requires both broad understanding across many files and deep reasoning about a tricky algorithm. What is a reasonable configuration?

options.text: Use plan mode for the breadth and raise the effort level for the depth.
options.explanation: The two features compose: plan mode handles broad multi-file understanding while a higher effort level handles the deep algorithmic reasoning.

options.text: Use only auto-accept mode, which covers both breadth and depth at once.
options.explanation: Auto-accept governs edit approval, not breadth or reasoning depth.

options.text: Use only the lowest effort level to keep the long task affordable.
options.explanation: Lowest effort undercuts the deep reasoning the algorithm needs.

options.text: Avoid both features altogether, since plan mode and a higher effort level cannot be combined in one session.
options.explanation: Combining plan mode and higher effort is explicitly supported.

questions.stem: When you start a fresh session in a project that has a project-root `CLAUDE.md`, how is that file used?

options.text: Claude reads it automatically at the start and appends it to your prompt.
options.explanation: Claude reads the project CLAUDE.md automatically at session start and appends its contents, acting like a persistent project system prompt.

options.text: It loads into the session only after you have explicitly run the `/init` command yourself.
options.explanation: `/init` generates the file; an existing one loads automatically without it.

options.text: It loads only when you mention a file in it with the `@` symbol.
options.explanation: The file loads regardless; @import additionally pulls referenced files.

options.text: It is ignored unless it is also copied into `.claude/settings.json`.
options.explanation: CLAUDE.md is read directly; it is not registered in settings.

questions.stem: An engineer wants a reusable code-reviewer that runs with fresh context and is restricted to read-only tools so it never edits files. What should they configure?

options.text: A subagent definition checked into the repo, scoped to read-only tools.
options.explanation: A code-reviewer subagent runs in its own fresh context and should be restricted to read-only tools and committed so the whole team uses the same reviewer.

options.text: A PostToolUse hook that automatically re-runs the project linter after every single file edit.
options.explanation: A linter hook formats or checks; it is not a fresh-context reviewer agent.

options.text: An MCP resource exposing the diff so the main agent reviews itself.
options.explanation: Self-review carries the main agent's bias and a resource is not a reviewer.

options.text: A user-level CLAUDE.md note asking the agent to be more careful.
options.explanation: A memory note does not create an isolated read-only reviewer.

questions.stem: While hand-rolling the agent loop, the model returns a `tool_use` block. After you execute the tool, how must you send the result back so the conversation stays valid?

options.text: Append a `tool_result` block keyed to the request's matching `tool_use_id`.
options.explanation: Each tool result must be a `tool_result` block carrying the same `tool_use_id` as the request so the model can correlate it with the call.

options.text: Append the output as a plain assistant text message.
options.explanation: Tool output must be a structured tool_result, not free assistant text.

options.text: Send the output as a brand-new system prompt for the next turn.
options.explanation: Results do not go in the system prompt; they are user-role tool_result blocks.

options.text: Discard the output and let the model re-derive it on the next call.
options.explanation: Discarding the result breaks the loop; the model needs the tool_result to proceed.

questions.stem: Your hand-built loop returns a response whose `stop_reason` is `end_turn`. What does this signal about the agentic loop?

options.text: The model wants another tool executed before it can continue.
options.explanation: A pending tool request is signaled by `tool_use`, not `end_turn`.

options.text: The model has now finished this turn and the loop should stop iterating.
options.explanation: `end_turn` means the model has completed its turn, so the loop terminates rather than executing more tools.

options.text: The request hit the token limit and must be retried.
options.explanation: A length cutoff has its own stop reason; `end_turn` is normal completion.

options.text: An error occurred and the loop should restart from the first prompt.
options.explanation: `end_turn` is a clean finish, not an error condition.

questions.stem: A response contains two `tool_use` blocks in a single assistant turn. How should the loop respond before the next model call?

options.text: Execute only the first tool and ignore the second to keep it simple.
options.explanation: Ignoring a requested tool leaves an unmatched tool_use and breaks the turn.

options.text: Merge both requests into one tool call and return a single result.
options.explanation: Each tool_use is distinct and needs its own correlated result.

options.text: Execute both tools and append a tool_result for each, keyed by its id.
options.explanation: Every tool_use block must get its own tool_result keyed to the matching tool_use_id before the next model call.

options.text: Stop the loop immediately because two tool calls in one turn indicate a malformed reply.
options.explanation: Multiple tool_use blocks are valid; the loop handles each one.

questions.stem: An agent's main context is filling with verbose exploration output from answering 'where are the auth endpoints?'. Which technique keeps the main context clean?

options.text: Delegate the exploration to a subagent that returns only a summary.
options.explanation: A subagent works in its own isolated context and returns just the answer, so the verbose exploration never clutters the main context.

options.text: Raise the effort level so the model reasons through clutter.
options.explanation: Effort level does not reduce how much exploration output enters context.

options.text: Read every candidate file in the main thread to settle it quickly.
options.explanation: Reading in the main thread is what fills the context in the first place.

options.text: Add the endpoints to `.mcp.json` so they no longer need exploring.
options.explanation: `.mcp.json` configures servers; it does not store discovered endpoints.

questions.stem: What is the defining property of a subagent's context relative to the main agent?

options.text: It shares the very same context window, so both agents see every piece of intermediate output produced.
options.explanation: Sharing context would defeat the purpose; subagents are isolated.

options.text: It operates in its own isolated context window separate from the main agent.
options.explanation: Each subagent runs in a completely separate context window, which is why only its summary returns to the main agent.

options.text: It can only ever run after the main agent has fully stopped.
options.explanation: Subagents run in parallel with the main agent, not strictly afterward.

options.text: It permanently inherits every tool result the main agent produced.
options.explanation: Isolated context means it does not inherit the main agent's tool results.

questions.stem: Why is a subagent recommended for a pre-push code review rather than asking the main agent to review its own work?

options.text: A subagent always runs noticeably faster than the main agent because it skips the agentic loop entirely.
options.explanation: Subagents still use the loop; the value is fresh, unbiased context.

options.text: A subagent can edit files directly while the main agent cannot.
options.explanation: A reviewer should be read-only; editing is not the reason to use one.

options.text: A subagent gives a fresh perspective without the main agent's session bias.
options.explanation: A reviewer subagent has fresh context and does not carry the bias the main agent built up while writing the code.

options.text: A subagent is the only component permitted to call the Bash tool.
options.explanation: Both can call Bash subject to permissions; that is not the rationale.

questions.stem: An engineer ended a session yesterday mid-refactor and wants to continue exactly where they left off today. Which capability does that?

options.text: Resuming the prior session so that its accumulated context is fully restored.
options.explanation: Resuming a session restores the prior conversation and context so work continues from where it stopped.

options.text: Running `/clear`, which starts fresh while keeping yesterday's memory.
options.explanation: `/clear` discards context; it does not resume the prior session.

options.text: Running `/init`, which reloads the previous conversation.
options.explanation: `/init` generates a CLAUDE.md; it does not restore a session.

options.text: Adding the work to `.mcp.json` so the next session reconnects to it.
options.explanation: `.mcp.json` configures servers; it does not persist a session's progress.

questions.stem: Using the Agent SDK, an engineer wants to branch from an existing session to try an alternative approach without disturbing the original. Which option supports this?

options.text: `fork_session`, which branches from the existing session into a new one.
options.explanation: Forking a session branches off the existing one, letting you explore an alternative path while preserving the original session.

options.text: `allowedTools`, which restricts the tool set the new branch may use.
options.explanation: `allowedTools` limits tools; it does not branch a session.

options.text: `/compact`, which copies the session into a summarized duplicate.
options.explanation: Compaction summarizes the current session; it does not fork a branch.

options.text: A PreToolUse hook, which automatically clones the entire conversation just before each and every tool call.
options.explanation: Hooks intercept tool calls; they do not fork sessions.

questions.stem: After resuming a session with `--resume`, the engineer had edited several files outside Claude in the meantime. What is the most effective first step?

options.text: Immediately ask for edits, trusting the resumed context.
options.explanation: The resumed context predates the external edits, so it may be stale.

options.text: Inform the resumed session which files changed so its context is current.
options.explanation: A resumed session holds a stale view, so telling it which files changed lets it re-read them and avoid acting on outdated content.

options.text: Fork the session first so the stale context is duplicated safely.
options.explanation: Forking copies the stale state; it does not refresh it.

options.text: Lower the effort level so the model relies less on remembered files.
options.explanation: Effort level does not update the session's stale file knowledge.

questions.stem: When Claude opens a PR via `gh pr create`, the session is linked to that PR. How does the engineer later resume work on that PR?

options.text: Re-run `/init` inside the PR branch to relink the session to it.
options.explanation: `/init` generates CLAUDE.md; it does not resume a PR-linked session.

options.text: Run `claude --from-pr <PR_NUMBER>` to pick up exactly where they left off.
options.explanation: `claude --from-pr <PR_NUMBER>` resumes the session linked to that PR, restoring context to address reviews or fix builds.

options.text: Open a brand-new session and paste the PR diff into the first prompt.
options.explanation: A fresh session loses the linked context that --from-pr restores.

options.text: Add the PR number to `.mcp.json` so the server reattaches it.
options.explanation: `.mcp.json` configures servers and has nothing to do with PR linkage.

questions.stem: An engineer wants the agent to think harder on a single difficult prompt without changing the session's effort level. What achieves that?

options.text: Include the keyword `ultrathink` in that prompt to signal extra reasoning.
options.explanation: The `ultrathink` keyword signals more reasoning for that single turn without altering the session-wide effort level.

options.text: Run `/effort max`, which only ever affects the one next prompt.
options.explanation: `/effort` changes the session's level persistently, not just one prompt.

options.text: Switch the session into plan mode, which forces extra-deep reasoning on every single reply.
options.explanation: Plan mode adds breadth and a planning gate, not per-prompt reasoning depth.

options.text: Add a Stop hook that re-prompts the model to reconsider its answer.
options.explanation: A Stop hook runs after responses; it is not a per-prompt reasoning signal.

questions.stem: A subagent is configured to preload a skill. How does skill loading differ inside a subagent compared to the main conversation?

options.text: The subagent loads the entire skill into context, unlike the main conversation.
options.explanation: When preloaded into a subagent, the whole skill is loaded into its context, whereas in the main conversation a skill loads its full contents only on demand.

options.text: The subagent loads only the skill's name, never its instructions.
options.explanation: Preloading puts the full skill into the subagent, not just the name.

options.text: The subagent cannot use skills at all and must use MCP tools instead.
options.explanation: Subagents can preload skills; they are not limited to MCP tools.

options.text: The subagent simply shares the main conversation's already-loaded skill state instead of loading its own.
options.explanation: Subagents have isolated context; they do not share loaded skill state.

questions.stem: An engineer hand-builds an SDK loop but forgets to feed each tool's output back before the next model call. What failure should they expect?

options.text: The model keeps acting as if the tool never ran, unable to use the result.
options.explanation: Without appending the tool_result keyed to its tool_use_id, the model has no record of the output and cannot progress on it.

options.text: The model automatically re-runs every tool until results appear.
options.explanation: The model does not auto-rerun tools; the loop owner must supply results.

options.text: The session immediately ends with a permanent permission error.
options.explanation: Missing results are a protocol gap, not a permission error.

options.text: The CLI silently switches to tool search mode to recover the output.
options.explanation: Tool search mode addresses context size of definitions, not missing results.

questions.stem: A team is connecting the agent to a cloud-based issue tracker that exposes a remote MCP endpoint. Which transport does the documentation recommend for this kind of remote, cloud service?

options.text: stdio, since it runs the server as a local subprocess.
options.explanation: stdio runs a local process; it is for tools needing direct system access, not a remote cloud service.

options.text: HTTP, the recommended and most widely supported remote transport.
options.explanation: HTTP servers are the recommended option for remote MCP servers and the most widely supported transport for cloud-based services.

options.text: SSE, the preferred transport for all new remote servers.
options.explanation: SSE is deprecated; the docs advise using HTTP instead where available.

options.text: WebSocket, the only transport that supports OAuth.
options.explanation: WebSocket supports neither OAuth nor the --transport flag; HTTP is the one that supports OAuth.

questions.stem: While adding a local stdio MCP server with `claude mcp add ... -- python server.py --port 8080`, an engineer asks why the `--` is required before the command. What is its purpose?

options.text: It separates Claude's own options from the command and the args that are passed on to the server.
options.explanation: For stdio servers, `--` separates Claude's options (like --transport, --env) from the server command and its arguments, which are passed untouched.

options.text: It tells Claude Code to launch the configured server in detached background mode at startup.
options.explanation: `--` does not control background execution; it is an argument separator.

options.text: It marks the server as project-scoped instead of local-scoped.
options.explanation: Scope is set with --scope, not the `--` separator.

options.text: It enables environment variable expansion for the args list.
options.explanation: Expansion is independent of `--`; the separator just divides Claude's flags from the server command.

questions.stem: The same MCP server is defined at local scope and at user scope with different URLs. Which definition does Claude Code use, and how are the entries combined?

options.text: User scope wins, and its fields are merged over the local entry.
options.explanation: Local scope has higher precedence than user scope, and entries are not merged.

options.text: Both definitions load at once as two completely separate connections to the same server.
options.explanation: When a server is defined in multiple places, Claude Code connects to it once, not twice.

options.text: Local scope wins, and the entire entry from that source is used.
options.explanation: Local scope has the highest precedence over project and user, and the entire entry from the winning source is used without merging fields across scopes.

options.text: The URLs are concatenated so both endpoints are tried in turn.
options.explanation: URLs are not concatenated; one source's complete entry is chosen by precedence.

questions.stem: A committed `.mcp.json` references `${API_KEY}` for a header, but a teammate has not set that variable and it has no default. What happens when Claude Code loads the config?

options.text: Claude Code fails to parse the config because the variable is unset.
options.explanation: If a required environment variable is not set and has no default value, Claude Code will fail to parse the config.

options.text: The header is silently dropped from the request and the server connects anyway without it.
options.explanation: An unset required variable with no default causes a parse failure, not a silent drop.

options.text: Claude Code substitutes an empty string for the missing variable.
options.explanation: An empty substitution only happens with a default like `${API_KEY:-}`; otherwise parsing fails.

options.text: Claude Code prompts the teammate to type the value interactively.
options.explanation: There is no interactive prompt for a missing variable; the config simply fails to parse.

questions.stem: With tool search enabled by default, a critical MCP server's tools must be visible to Claude on every turn without a search step. Which configuration achieves that for just that server?

options.text: Set `ENABLE_TOOL_SEARCH=false` so all servers load upfront.
options.explanation: This disables deferral for every server, not just the one that needs to be always visible.

options.text: Add `"alwaysLoad": true` to that server's `.mcp.json` entry.
options.explanation: Setting `alwaysLoad: true` on a server loads all its tools into context at session start regardless of the tool search setting, leaving other servers deferred.

options.text: Move the server into user scope so that its tools are prioritized over the others.
options.explanation: Scope controls sharing and precedence, not whether tools are always loaded into context.

options.text: Add the server name to the permissions deny list.
options.explanation: A deny rule blocks the tool; it does not make it always visible.

questions.stem: An engineer is choosing between the Anthropic Client SDK and the Claude Agent SDK to build a code-exploration agent. What is the key difference in how tool execution is handled?

options.text: With the Client SDK you implement the tool loop; the Agent SDK handles tools for you.
options.explanation: The Client SDK gives direct API access where you implement the tool loop yourself, while the Agent SDK provides built-in tool execution and runs the agent loop.

options.text: The Client SDK ships built-in Read and Bash tools, whereas the Agent SDK ships none of them at all.
options.explanation: It is reversed: the Agent SDK provides built-in tools; the Client SDK requires you to implement execution.

options.text: Both SDKs execute tools on Anthropic's servers with no local code.
options.explanation: Client tools execute in your application; neither SDK runs your tools entirely on Anthropic servers.

options.text: Only the Client SDK can connect to MCP servers.
options.explanation: The Agent SDK supports MCP servers too; MCP is not exclusive to the Client SDK.

questions.stem: An MCP tool runs on Anthropic's infrastructure as a server tool. How does this differ from a client tool in the agentic loop?

options.text: A server tool still requires your application code to send back a `tool_result` block for each call it makes.
options.explanation: Returning a tool_result is the client-tool flow; server tools are executed by Anthropic.

options.text: For a server tool, Anthropic executes it and you see the results directly.
options.explanation: Server tools run on Anthropic's infrastructure and return results directly, whereas client tools stop with tool_use for your code to execute and return a tool_result.

options.text: A server tool always stops the loop with `stop_reason: end_turn`.
options.explanation: Server-tool execution does not force end_turn; that reason signals turn completion, not server execution.

options.text: A server tool cannot appear in the same request as any client tool.
options.explanation: There is no such restriction; the distinction is only where execution happens.

questions.stem: In the Agent SDK, an engineer wants to resume a previous query with full prior context. Where do they obtain the identifier needed to resume?

options.text: From the `session_id` carried on the init system message emitted during the very first query.
options.explanation: The first query emits an init system message carrying the session_id, which you pass as `resume` to continue with full prior context.

options.text: From the `allowed_tools` option that was set when the first query was originally issued.
options.explanation: `allowed_tools` configures tool access, not a session identifier.

options.text: From the project's `.mcp.json` server entry.
options.explanation: `.mcp.json` configures MCP servers and holds no session id.

options.text: From the contents of the project root `CLAUDE.md`.
options.explanation: CLAUDE.md provides memory, not a session identifier to resume.

questions.stem: A connected MCP server exposes prompts, and the team wants to invoke one named `pr_review` for issue 456. How does that prompt surface in Claude Code?

options.text: As an @-mention resource referenced like `@github:pr_review`.
options.explanation: @-mentions reference resources; prompts surface as slash commands, not resource mentions.

options.text: As an entry that must first be added to `.claude/commands/`.
options.explanation: MCP prompts are discovered dynamically; you do not copy them into the commands directory.

options.text: As a slash command written like `/mcp__github__pr_review 456`.
options.explanation: MCP prompts appear as commands in the form `/mcp__servername__promptname`, with arguments passed space-separated after the command.

options.text: As a new built-in tool automatically named `pr_review`.
options.explanation: Prompts become slash commands, not built-in tools.

questions.stem: An MCP docs server exposes an API reference resource, and an engineer wants to point the agent at it inline in a prompt. What is the correct way to reference it?

options.text: Add the resource path to the `args` array in `.mcp.json`.
options.explanation: `args` configures a server command; it is not how you reference a resource in a prompt.

options.text: Use an @ mention inline, written like `@docs:file://api/authentication`.
options.explanation: MCP resources are referenced with @ mentions in the form `@server:protocol://resource/path`, and are fetched and attached automatically.

options.text: Invoke it as a slash command `/docs api/authentication`.
options.explanation: Slash commands are for MCP prompts; resources are referenced via @ mentions.

options.text: Paste the resource URI into the project `CLAUDE.md` deny list.
options.explanation: There is no resource deny list in CLAUDE.md; resources are @-mentioned in prompts.

questions.stem: When adding an MCP server with `claude mcp add`, which `--scope` value shares the server with the whole team via a version-controlled file?

options.text: `local`, the default scope for the current project.
options.explanation: Local scope is private to you in the current project and is not shared via version control.

options.text: `user`, which spans all of your projects.
options.explanation: User scope is available across your projects but remains private, not team-shared.

options.text: `project`, which writes the server into a shared `.mcp.json`.
options.explanation: The `project` scope stores the server in `.mcp.json` at the project root, which is checked into version control and shared with everyone on the team.

options.text: `enterprise`, which each individual developer selects manually at install time on their machine.
options.explanation: Enterprise-level managed configuration is deployed by administrators, not chosen per developer to share a team server.

questions.stem: An agent is not calling the available investigation tools and instead answers a codebase question from memory. Per the docs, what is a reliable steering technique?

options.text: Add a system-prompt instruction like "Use the tools to investigate before responding."
options.explanation: A light system-prompt instruction such as "Use the tools to investigate before responding" measurably increases tool use when Claude is not calling tools as expected.

options.text: Remove all tool descriptions so Claude must explore to learn them.
options.explanation: Stripping descriptions degrades correct tool selection; clear descriptions are what guide use.

options.text: Lower the `max_tokens` setting so the model has no remaining room left to answer from its own memory.
options.explanation: Reducing output budget truncates answers; it does not steer toward tool use.

options.text: Delete the project `CLAUDE.md` so the agent has less context.
options.explanation: Removing memory does not encourage tool use; a system-prompt nudge does.

questions.stem: An engineer needs to find every file in a large monorepo whose contents call the deprecated function `legacyParse(` so they can plan a migration. Which built-in tool is the right choice?

options.text: Grep, to search file contents for the `legacyParse(` pattern across the repo.
options.explanation: Grep searches file contents by pattern — the right tool for locating occurrences of a function call across many files.

options.text: Glob, with a pattern like `**/*legacy*` to find files by name.
options.explanation: Glob finds files by name/path, but the call sites are not necessarily in files named 'legacy'; content search is needed.

options.text: Read, opening each file and scanning for the call.
options.explanation: Reading every file individually is far less efficient than a content search; Read is for known specific files.

options.text: Bash, running `ls -R` and eyeballing the output.
options.explanation: Listing files does not search their contents; a specialized content-search tool (Grep) is preferred over ad hoc Bash here.

questions.stem: An engineer wants to locate all TypeScript test files in the repo, which follow the naming convention `*.test.ts`, before running a coverage pass. Which built-in tool is most appropriate?

options.text: Glob, with a pattern like `**/*.test.ts` to find files by their naming convention.
options.explanation: Glob finds files by name/path pattern — exactly right for discovering files matching a naming convention like `*.test.ts`.

options.text: Grep, to search contents for the word 'test'.
options.explanation: Grep searches contents, which would match unrelated occurrences of 'test' and miss the goal of finding files by name.

options.text: Read, opening the test directory's index file first and then following it to each individual test module in turn.
options.explanation: Read targets a known file; it does not discover files by pattern across the tree.

options.text: Bash, running a custom `find` with `sed` post-processing.
options.explanation: A specialized file-discovery tool (Glob) is preferred over ad hoc Bash for tasks it handles directly.

questions.stem: You added an MCP server exposing `analyze_dependencies`, but the agent keeps using Grep to find imports even when asked about dependencies. The MCP tool's description reads only "Analyzes dependency graph." What is the most effective fix?

options.text: Expand the description to detail what it builds and returns, e.g. "Builds a dependency graph of direct imports, transitive deps, and cycles."
options.explanation: Tool descriptions are the primary selection signal. When Claude misselects, the first fix is a richer description detailing capabilities and outputs so the model prefers it over Grep.

options.text: Remove Grep from the agent so it is forced to use the MCP tool.
options.explanation: Removing useful built-ins is a heavy-handed move; the documented first step is improving the description, not pruning tools.

options.text: Split `analyze_dependencies` into three smaller tools immediately.
options.explanation: Consolidation/splitting comes after improving descriptions and examples — never the first step for misselection.

options.text: Add a system-prompt routing rule stating that all dependency-related questions must be sent to the MCP tool rather than Grep.
options.explanation: Prompt routing is probabilistic and secondary; the primary lever is the tool description itself.

questions.stem: An engineer asks the agent to investigate why one API endpoint intermittently returns 500s in a 200-file codebase, with no idea which components are involved. How should the agent decompose the investigation?

options.text: Dynamically generate investigation subtasks based on what it finds at each step, adapting as the error path emerges.
options.explanation: When the decomposition is unknown upfront, dynamic adaptive decomposition lets the agent follow the evidence, generating subtasks as it learns the error path.

options.text: Fix a rigid step sequence (grep, read handlers, check queries, examine middleware) and run it regardless of findings.
options.explanation: A fixed sequence ignores intermediate findings and is brittle for an unknown error path; dynamic adaptation fits better.

options.text: Produce a complete plan mapping all code paths before reading any file.
options.explanation: Planning every path upfront in an unfamiliar 200-file codebase is impractical and wasteful before exploration reveals the relevant components.

options.text: Launch parallel agents on all four layers at once and merge results.
options.explanation: Fanning out to all layers before knowing which is implicated burns effort; the path should be discovered adaptively.

questions.stem: The agent has read 8 of 45 files while mapping untested code in a legacy payments module and is already forgetting earlier patterns before locating all the test files. What approach best completes the investigation?

options.text: Spawn subagents for focused questions (find all payment test files, trace the refund flow) while the main agent coordinates and keeps the high-level picture.
options.explanation: Delegating focused sub-investigations to subagents keeps each context small and lets the coordinator preserve overall understanding — the right move when one context is degrading under load.

options.text: Keep reading all 45 files within the single conversation to build a complete function inventory before writing anything down or delegating.
options.explanation: Cramming all files into one degrading context worsens attention dilution and loss of earlier detail.

options.text: Switch entirely to Grep and stop reading whole files to save context, searching only for specific function names from here on.
options.explanation: Abandoning reading loses the structural understanding needed to trace flows; the issue is context strategy, not tool choice.

options.text: Summarize findings, then `/clear` and rely solely on the summary.
options.explanation: A lossy summary as the sole reference drops specific details; subagents plus a coordinator preserve fidelity better.

questions.stem: A new engineer keeps having to re-explain the project's build commands, directory layout, and naming conventions to Claude Code at the start of every session. What is the right way to make this context available automatically on every run?

options.text: Document it in the project's `CLAUDE.md` so Claude Code loads it as context every session.
options.explanation: CLAUDE.md provides persistent project context that Claude Code reads automatically each session — the right home for build commands, layout, and conventions.

options.text: Paste the context into the first message of each session manually.
options.explanation: Manual pasting is exactly the repetition CLAUDE.md eliminates and is error-prone.

options.text: Put it in a personal slash command the engineer must remember to run.
options.explanation: A command requires explicit invocation; project context should load automatically via CLAUDE.md.

options.text: Store the project context inside a PreToolUse hook that injects it whenever a tool is about to run during the session.
options.explanation: Hooks enforce or validate tool calls; they are not the mechanism for supplying always-on project context.

questions.stem: The agent needs to cross-reference code changes with the team's Jira tickets during reviews — currently done by copy-pasting ticket text into the conversation. The team wants the agent to access standard Jira ticket data directly. What is the most effective approach?

options.text: Integrate an existing Jira MCP server that exposes tickets, comments, and metadata through discoverable tool interfaces.
options.explanation: Jira is a common integration; adopting an existing community MCP server gives the agent live, discoverable access to ticket data without bespoke maintenance.

options.text: Build a custom MCP server from scratch wrapping Jira's API.
options.explanation: Custom-building is the last resort; you check for a community server first, and Jira is well served.

options.text: Use Bash `curl` against Jira's REST API with inline auth and JSON parsing each time.
options.explanation: Ad hoc curl bypasses structured, discoverable tool interfaces and robust error handling that an MCP server provides.

options.text: Export the tickets to markdown files in the repository on a nightly schedule and have the agent read those files during review.
options.explanation: Static exports go stale immediately and cannot reflect recent comments or status changes the review needs.

questions.stem: An engineer asks Claude Code to rename a single private helper function and update its three call sites in one file — a small, unambiguous change. Which working mode is appropriate?

options.text: Direct execution, because the scope is clear and well-defined with no ambiguity about approach.
options.explanation: Direct execution fits clear, well-scoped tasks with one obvious approach; plan mode would add overhead with no benefit.

options.text: Plan mode, to outline the rename before doing it.
options.explanation: Plan mode is for complex tasks with multiple approaches or high consequences; a trivial local rename does not need it.

options.text: The `-p` flag, to run it non-interactively.
options.explanation: `-p` is for CI/CD non-interactive runs, not the deciding factor for an interactive one-line rename.

options.text: Run an independent review session to evaluate the rename before any of the call sites are actually modified in the file.
options.explanation: Independent review applies to evaluating generated code, not to executing a trivial, unambiguous edit.

questions.stem: An engineer resumes yesterday's investigation session by ID. Version control shows 3 of the 12 files the agent had read were changed overnight by a teammate's merge. What approach best balances efficiency and accuracy?

options.text: Resume the session and tell the agent exactly which 3 files changed so it re-reads only those.
options.explanation: Resuming preserves accumulated context (efficiency) while informing the agent of the 3 changed files triggers targeted re-analysis (accuracy) — the best balance.

options.text: Resume without mentioning the changes.
options.explanation: Leaving the agent unaware lets it reason from stale content for the modified files, risking wrong conclusions.

options.text: Resume the saved session and immediately have the agent re-read all twelve previously analyzed files before continuing the work.
options.explanation: Re-reading everything wastes effort on the 9 unchanged files; only the 3 changed ones need re-analysis.

options.text: Discard the session and start fresh.
options.explanation: Starting over throws away an hour of valid context for the 9 unchanged files — needlessly inefficient.

questions.stem: You configured three MCP servers for the agent — git, Jira, and docs search. A user asks it to "create a branch for JIRA-123 and add documentation links to the ticket." How does the agent access tools spanning these servers?

options.text: Tools from all configured MCP servers are discovered at connection time and available simultaneously.
options.explanation: All configured servers' tools are discovered when connections are established and are available to the agent at once; it can mix git, Jira, and docs tools in one task.

options.text: You must pick one server per turn; the agent can use only that server's tools at a time.
options.explanation: There is no one-server-per-turn restriction; tools across servers are concurrently available.

options.text: The agent loads only the single most relevant server's tools per request.
options.explanation: The agent does not restrict itself to one server; this task explicitly spans git, Jira, and docs simultaneously.

options.text: The agent queries servers sequentially to discover which owns each tool by name prefix.
options.explanation: Discovery happens at connection time, not via sequential per-call probing; tools are already known and available.
